rulings.Case Title: PATRICK CORKERY VS. JEAN SHIOMOTO
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Grant Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 3, 2019, Line 10, PETITIONER PATRICK CORKERY's Motion To Grant Petition For Writ Of Administrative Mandamus. Petitioner Corkery's petition for writ of administrative mandamus is denied. The court rejects petitioner's timeliness argument. It appears that respondent received the complete record by August 6, 2019, but the date of receipt is not entirely clear. (AR, 64.) Petitioner argues that respondent's answer was filed more than 30 days later, was untimely under Code of Civ. Proc. 1089.5, and petitioner's allegations are therefore deemed admitted. The court rejects this argument. First, the argument was made for the first time in the reply papers and there was nothing that prevented petitioner from raising this issue in his initial moving papers. (See Hernandez v. First Student, Inc. (2019) 37 Cal.App.4th 270, 277 ["[w]e will not ordinarily consider issues raised for the first time in a reply brief."]; American Drug Stores, Inc. v. Stroh (1992) 10 Cal.App.4th 1446, 1453 ["Points raised for the first time in a reply brief will ordinarily not be considered, because such consideration would deprive the respondent of an opportunity to counter the argument."].) The court notes that respondent's answer was filed on September 9, 2019, and that was the same date the petitioner filed his notice of motion. Petitioner was in possession of AR 64 when he filed his moving papers, but failed to raise the timeliness issue. Second, petitioner's authorities stand for the proposition that the allegations of the petition are taken as true where no answer is filed, not where an untimely answer is filed. (See Block v. Superior Court (1998) 62 Cal.App.4th 363, 368, fn. 2 ["Failure to answer the petition is considered an admission of the truth of the allegations."] Exercising its independent judgment, the court concludes that the weight of the evidence supports respondent's decision. The hearing officer reasonably concluded that the chemical admonition was provided, and petitioner refused to comply even though the DS-367 and collision report are not entirely consistent. The DS-367 indicates that Officer Hoang provided the admonition. The collision report indicates that Officer Tsang provided the admonition (AR 20). The court concludes that Officer Tsang provided the admonition at the station and Hoang was present to observe it since both Tsang and Hoang transported petitioner to the police station. (AR 20.) Hoang's shifting statements were the result of sloppy paperwork, not perjury. Administrative hearings are not conducted with the same strictness as a court proceeding. (See Gov't. Code, § 11513(c) ["The hearing need not be conducted according to technical rules relating to evidence"]; MacDonald v. Gutierrez (2004) 32 Cal.4th 150, 153 and 159 [hearing officer can consider unsworn officer statement]; Lake v. Reed (1997) 16 Cal.4th 448, 461 ["A police officer's report, even if unsworn, constitutes 'the sort of evidence on which responsible persons are accustomed to rely in the conduct of serious affairs.' "].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. rulings.Case Title: CHAO HONG TAN VS. JUSTIN CHEUNG ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 3, 2019, Line 2, DEFENDANT JUSTIN CHEUNG, KELVIN CHEUNG, VILMA CHEUNG's DEMURRER TO 2ND AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants Justin, Kelvin, and Vilma Cheung's demurrer to the second amended complaint is sustained in part and overruled in part. The demurrers to the conversion causes of action (1-3) are overruled. Whether plaintiff can present clear and convincing evidence that Stanley intended for the funds and other property to go to plaintiff and whether the funds are sufficiently identifiable are factual issues that cannot be resolved on demurrer. The face of the complaint does not reveal that the claims are time-barred. A demurrer can be used only to challenge defects that appear on the face of the complaint or matters outside the pleading that are judicially noticeable; the court does not consider discovery responses or other extrinsic evidence. Both parties' requests for judicial notice of discovery responses and related motions are denied. The demurrer to the accounting cause of action is overruled. "A cause of action for an accounting requires a showing that a relationship exists between the plaintiff and defendant that requires an accounting, and that some balance is due the plaintiff that can only be ascertained by an accounting." (Jolley v. Chase Home Finance, LLC (2013) 213 Cal.App.4th 872, 910.) As the administrator of Stanley's estate, plaintiff sufficiently alleges a relationship between the parties to support the claim. The demurrer to the UCL cause of action is sustained with leave to amend. If plaintiff seeks to allege an unlawful business practice, she needs to identify the statutes that were breached. (See Khoury v. Maly's of California, Inc. (1993) 14 Cal.App.4th 612, 619 ["A plaintiff alleging unfair business practices under these statutes must state with reasonable particularity the facts supporting the statutory elements of the violation."].) The demurrer to the declaratory relief cause of action is overruled. Lastly, plaintiff has leave to plead the civil conspiracy, unjust enrichment, and injunctive relief allegations as remedies and/or general charging allegations. They do not constitute separate causes of action. Defendants' remaining arguments lack merit. The financial institutions and insurance carriers are not indispensable parties, nor is the second amended complaint fatally uncertain. No breach of contract is alleged. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: JUSTIN FRANCE VS. MERCEDES-BENZ USA, LLC ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Plaintiffs Notice Of Motion And Motion For Order To Enforce Settlement Agreement; Memo Of Points And Authorities
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 3, 2019, Line 11, PLAINTIFF JUSTIN FRANCE's Motion For Order To Enforce Settlement Agreement. Plaintiff's motion to enforce the settlement agreement is granted. The parties do not dispute that the settlement agreement was in writing with the parties' signatures. (See Code Civ. Proc., § 664.6.) A settlement agreement is interpreted according to the same principles as any other written agreement. (Leeman v. Adams Extract & Spice, LLC (2015) 236 Cal.App.4th 1367, 1374 ["If the language of the agreement is clear and explicit and does not involve an absurdity, determination of the mutual intent of the parties and interpretation of the contract is to be based on the language of the agreement alone."].) Defendant argues the settlement agreement should be voided or, in the alternative, reformed, due to an alleged mistake of fact or fraud. However, the Court finds the settlement agreement to be clear and unambiguous. Further, the settlement agreement contains an integration clause which provides that there are no prior or contemporaneous understandings or agreements of the parties. As the record reflects, the parties, both represented by counsel, negotiated for an entire year after the section 998 offer to compromise. (Khan Decl., 3; Turzak Decl., Ex. A.) In the parties' efforts to compromise, the agreed-upon settlement figure was negotiated down from $81,593.88 to the eventual agreed-upon payment of $54,230.14. (Khan Decl., Ex. B.) Defendant had ample opportunity to verify the figures and conduct its own investigation, and indeed admits that if it had simply noted the dates of the four contested payments, it would have realized that they were made prior to Plaintiff leasing the subject vehicle. (Opp. at 5.) Defendant has not shown that the agreement is unconscionable or unduly unfavorable to it. Donovan v. RRL Corp. (2001) 26 Cal.4th 261 is distinguishable as it involved a contract between a consumer and car dealership, where the enforcement of the contract would be unconscionable in light of the magnitude of the parties' mistake. (Id.) Here, the agreement was negotiated by counsel over the period of one year, resulting in a compromise and a signed settlement agreement. Defendant does not provide any evidence that the calculated payments in the settlement agreement are erroneous or were anything but the product of careful negotiations and thoughtful compromise. Plaintiff's motion to enforce the settlement agreement is granted, and the court will enter judgment pursuant to the terms of the settlement. Pursuant to paragraph 13 of the parties' settlement agreement, Plaintiff may seek an award of its reasonable attorney's fees and costs incurred in connection with this motion. The parties are ordered to meet and confer in an attempt to resolve Plaintiff's entitlement to reasonable attorney's fees and costs pursuant to Civ. Code §1794(d) and paragraph 3(E) of the settlement agreement. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the Plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and a proposed judgment enforcing the settlement agreement and must bring them to the hearing or email them to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: LAWRENCE GOMEZ ET AL VS. THE INN ON BROADWAY ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 3, 2019, Line 6, DEFENDANT TRIKON MANAGEMENT, INC., JAI LAXMI, LLC's MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Or, In The Alternative, Summary Adjudication. Defendants Trikon Management, Inc. and Jai Laxmi, LLC's motion for summary judgment is denied. Defendant fails to maintain its initial burden of production. The owner-operator of an elevator is a common carrier and it owes a high duty of care to those that use the machine. (See Treadwell v. Whittier (1889) 80 Cal. 574, 600 [elevator owners "must adopt the most improved modes of construction and machinery in known use in the business, and if they do not, and injury occur, they will be held responsible."]; Brown v. George Pepperdine Foundation (1943) 23 Cal.2d 256, 261 ["An owner is bound to use the utmost care and diligence in the maintenance of elevators. [Citations.] In the fulfillment of this obligation something more than regular and frequent inspections is required."] In this case, defendant argues that it conducted regular inspections and had no actual or constructive notice of a dangerous condition. Defendant fails to maintain its burden of production. Defendant fails to show that it met its heightened duty in maintaining the elevator. Defendant admits that the pump tank nut was missing on June 2, 2016, and it was unclear whether it fell into the tank. Mr. Bruce Agid opines that the nut falling into the tank would not create a dangerous condition. The court sustains plaintiff's objection to this testimony because Mr. Agid has not been properly qualified as an expert witness. The court further notes that defendants fail to adduce any "state all facts" discovery responses that would allow the court to reasonably infer that no other defects existed in the elevator and plaintiffs cannot establish their claims. (See Scheiding v. Dinwiddie (1999) 69 Cal.App.4th 64, 84 [motion that did not rely upon "factually devoid" discovery responses from which an absence of evidence could be inferred was "insufficient to support a reasonable inference that plaintiffs could not produce further evidence to support an element crucial to their claims."]; Weber v. John Crane (2006) 143 Cal.App.4th 1433, 1439 [burden did not shift where defendant relied solely on plaintiff's deposition testimony and did not cite any of plaintiff's special discovery responses].) Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: GEORGIA HERDENER VS. TAPJOY, INC., A DELAWARE CORPORATION ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Defendant Tapjoy, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or Summary Adjudication
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 3, 2019, Line 14, Defendant Tapjoy, Inc.'S Motion For Summary Judgment Or Summary Adjudication. Defendants' motion for summary judgment is granted. Defendants' defense of after-acquired evidence does not bar Plaintiff's claims, as it only limits Plaintiff's remedies, rather than disposing of an entire cause of action, as is required for summary adjudication. Under Cal. Civ. Proc. § 437c(f)(1), a "motion for summary adjudication shall be granted only if it completely disposes of a cause of action, an affirmative defense, a claim for damages, or an issue of duty." The doctrines of after-acquired evidence and unclean hands are "not complete defenses to a worker's claims under California's FEHA, although they do affect the availability of remedies." (Salas v. Sierra Chemical Co. (2014) 59 Cal.4th 407, 414; see also Horne v. District Council 16 Internat. Union of Painters & Allied Trades (2015) 234 Cal.App.4th 524, 537-541[trial court erred in relying upon after-acquired evidence in granting summary judgment for employer in employment discrimination action].) The Court's full tentative ruling is emailed to the parties. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the defendant is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested.=(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: ASHLEY HOWITT VS. ZEPHYR REAL ESTATE ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Motion For Relief Of Evidentiary Sanctions (9/16 Ost)
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 3, 2019, Line 9, Motion For Relief Of Evidentiary Sanctions (9/16 Ost). Plaintiff's motion for an order relieving her from the evidentiary sanctions and waiver of her objections to discovery imposed by the court's April 9 and June 20, 2019 orders is denied. This action was filed on September 24, 2018. On October 25, 2018, defendants served a request for production of documents on plaintiff. On November 9, plaintiff sought and was granted a 30-day extension of time to respond to that RFP, until December 30, 2018. However, plaintiff did not timely respond, and correspondence from defendants' counsel (dated January 10 and February 15, 2019) inquiring as to the status of plaintiff's discovery responses went unanswered. rulings.Case Title: VINITA BUSSE VS. ANDREW G. PRICE, INDIVIDUALLY, AND D/B/A ARTISANAL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Set for hearing on Thursday, October 3, 2019, Line 13, DEFENDANT ANDREW G. PRICE, INDIVIDUALLY, AND D/B/A ARTISANAL, ARTISANAL TALENT GROUP, INC, A CALIFORNIA's MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT. Defendants' motion to strike is granted in part with leave to amend. Defendants fail to show that Plaintiff's demand for "disgorgement" is an improper claim for relief under the UCL. "Under the UCL, an individual may recover profits unfairly obtained to the extent that these profits represent monies given to the defendant or benefits in which the plaintiff has an ownership interest." (Korea Supply Co. v. Lockheed Martin (2003) 29 Cal.4th 1134, 1148 [explaining that only "nonrestitutionary disgorgement of profits" is unavailable as a remedy under the UCL].) Defendants' motion to strike the allegations in 39 of the FAC is granted, with leave to amend, as to Plaintiff's allegations that Defendants' representations that Plaintiff would be hired and held out as founding partner were false. Plaintiff in her complaint admits that she "entered into employment." (FAC 21.) And, in responding to discovery, Plaintiff admits that she "held the title of Founding Partner." (Pl.'s Resp. to Defs.' Req. for Admis. 6.) Defendants' motion to strike plaintiff's prayer for attorney's fees under Code Civ. Proc. § 1021.5 is granted as unopposed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. rulings.Case Title: CONCEPCION SILVA VS. JOSE ANTONIO ESCOLERO ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion For An Order Establishing Admissions
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Wednesday, October 2, 2019, Line 5, PLAINTIFF CONCEPCION SILVA's Motion For An Order Establishing Admissions. Pro Tem Judge Roger Mead, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Plaintiffs' unopposed motion is granted. Defendant Jose Antonio Escolero, a/k/a Jose Antonio Escolero Bonillo, has waived all objections to, and is deemed to have admitted the truth of all matters specified in, Plaintiff Concepcion Silva's Request for Admissions To Defendant Jose Antonio Escolero Bonilla - Set Two, dated July 26, 2019. If the tentative ruling is not contested, Plaintiffs' counsel may submit the proposed order by e-mail to the Judge Pro Tem before the hearing and need not appear; the proposed order should include the Requests for Admission that are deemed admitted as an exhibit. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to rmead@folgerlevin.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. =(302/JPT)
